answers.body:
You would need to check it yourself, using an SNTP client or something.

There is no requirement that a device use "the Internet" for automatically setting the time, even if the user has not manually changed the time. For example, many devices in the US rely on NITZ signals from the wireless carriers as their time source.

It seems that there's already a similar question here: How to use an Internet time server to get the time?

I must admit I haven't personaly used this, but it looks like it's worth a try. It uses apache commons library.

Copying relevant code (full explanation on the link): 

String TIME_SERVER = "time-a.nist.gov";   
NTPUDPClient timeClient = new NTPUDPClient();
InetAddress inetAddress = InetAddress.getByName(TIME_SERVER);
TimeInfo timeInfo = timeClient.getTime(inetAddress);
long returnTime = timeInfo.getMessage().getTransmitTimeStamp().getTime();
Date time = new Date(returnTime);

Something it doesn't say there is that the TIME_SERVER may not be updated. Here's a list of servers with their availability: http://tf.nist.gov/tf-cgi/servers.cgi#

Please let me know if this worked for you :)

comments.body: Depending on a third party website to put the current time in the date header is a really risky (caching exists!!) strategy (especially when the NTP protocol exists). The decade old accepted answer to this question is a much more sensible approach to solving the problem.
Also, please avoid code-only answers that don't explain anything about how they solve the problem.

Your answer could be improved with additional supporting information. Please edit to add further details, such as citations or documentation, so that others can confirm that your answer is correct. You can find more information on how to write good answers in the help center.

Never ever use Date, DateFormat nor SmpleDateFormat in new code. Those classes were notoriously troublesome and fortunately have been obsolete since Java 8 and java.time, the modern Java date and time API. So more than 11 years and counting.
@Anonymous You can use other methods, but I prefer to stick with the old ones :)
@Anonymous Sorry, I previously cut the script I wrote. I've now changed all the scripts I've included.

@Quentin How should I explain this from the beginning? My English is very little. :)  W32Time on Windows gets the network time from time.windows.com in the command prompt "w32tm /resync /rediscover." I use google.co.id because it's the fastest website and a major company.  You can use your own website or switch to time.windows.com, I think it will all work.

answers.body:
This method does use a third-party website. You can use your own website or switch to time.windows.com, I think it will all work.

java.util.Date datTim = null;
try {
   java.net.URL url = new java.net.URL("http://www.google.co.id");
   java.net.HttpURLConnection http = (java.net.HttpURLConnection)
   url.openConnection();
   String respone1 = http.getHeaderField("Date");
   java.text.DateFormat df = new java.text.SimpleDateFormat("EEE, dd MMM yyyy HH:mm:ss z");

//==== Methode 1 ===================================================
  DateTimeFormatter dateTimeFormatter = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("EEE, dd MMM yyyy HH:mm:ss z");
  LocalDateTime dateTime = LocalDateTime.parse(respone1, dateTimeFormatter);
  @SuppressWarnings("static-access")
  java.util.Date date = (java.util.Date) java.util.Date.from(dateTime.atZone(ZoneId.of("GMT").systemDefault()).toInstant());
  System.out.println("LocalDateTime: " + dateTime);
  System.out.println("java.util.Date: " + date);

//==== Methode 2 ===================================================
   df.setTimeZone(java.util.TimeZone.getTimeZone("GMT"));
   System.out.println(df.format(new java.util.Date()));
   datTim = (java.util.Date) df.parse(respone1);
   System.out.println(df.format(datTim));

} catch (java.io.IOException | java.text.ParseException ex) {
   javax.swing.JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(null, ex.getMessage());
}

title: Make sure time is provided by Internet
body: I want to use the current online time in my Android application. I know that you can make sure that the time is provided by the Internet in the Settings of Android. How can I check that in Java?
